surface.name: Asana REST API Quick start guide with a personal access token
surface.selection_basis: Asana's developer home directs new API users to its step-by-step Quick start. The platform overview calls the REST API its primary platform feature and says most developers use a personal access token for first API calls. The Quick start then performs create, read, update, and delete operations on one task.

alternatives_considered.name: Asana API explorer
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The platform overview lists the explorer as an alternative request interface, while the developer home explicitly promotes the Quick start for a first app and token.

alternatives_considered.name: OAuth application
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Asana recommends OAuth for applications acting for users, but the platform overview says most developers test their first calls with a personal access token.

alternatives_considered.name: Developer sandbox
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: A sandbox is an optional test workspace for premium features; the Quick start can use any accessible workspace.

entry_point.developer_intent: Create or access an Asana workspace, generate a personal access token, obtain the workspace GID, and complete the Quick start's task create, read, update, and delete sequence.
assumed_prior_state: No Asana account, organization or workspace membership, team, developer sandbox, developer-console access, personal access token, workspace GID, task, or prior API request is assumed.
A browser, email or supported identity-provider account, terminal, cURL, and basic REST knowledge are available.
No account was created, identity provider was authorized, invitation was accepted, workspace was created or joined, token was generated, or API request was sent during research.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Complete the Quick start task CRUD operations and become ready to begin development against the Asana API.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: The intended terminal is an authenticated sequence that creates `Sample task`, retrieves it, renames it to `Modified task`, deletes it, and reaches the guide's readiness statement.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The guide's final delete returns `200 OK` with an empty data object, after earlier `201 Created` and `200 OK` responses, then states that the developer is ready to begin API development.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The API terminal is demonstrated, but the no-account transition through current authentication, workspace creation or membership, and developer-console access is not fully public.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: The Quick start places Next steps only after task CRUD. It requires an Asana account and accessible workspace, but the current developer-console link redirects to login and the account guide does not resolve every identity and workspace branch to the console.

prerequisites.requirement: Create or access an Asana account tied to the developer's identity.
Have an existing accessible workspace, create a free account and workspace, or request a developer sandbox.
Create and securely copy a personal access token from the developer console.
Obtain the GID of the workspace in which the sample task will be created.
Have cURL and basic REST knowledge for the selected command-line examples.

primary_path.action: Open Asana developer documentation and select the promoted step-by-step Quick start.
primary_path.output: The developer home identifies the Quick start as the first-app guide for token creation and API task creation.
primary_path.success_signal: The Quick start link is available.

primary_path.action: Open the Quick start and follow its first setup action to the developer console.
primary_path.output: The guide requires a personal access token and workspace GID before its task requests.
primary_path.success_signal: The developer-console link and token instructions are visible.

primary_path.action: Open the developer console from a no-account state.
primary_path.output: Asana redirects to a login page offering Google, Microsoft, or email continuation.
primary_path.success_signal: The initial identity choices are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Research stops because the selected identity flow, verification, password or magic-link state, corporate-domain organization branch, workspace or team creation or join, and return to the developer console are not fully enumerated by one current public path.

candidate_path_gap.reason: The current login page shows Google, Microsoft, and email, while the account guide describes email and password, Google, SAML, invitation, and corporate-domain outcomes without giving one complete no-account route through verification and workspace convergence to the developer console.
candidate_path_gap.impact: A personal access token and workspace GID cannot be reached reproducibly from no account, so the documented CRUD route has no complete public candidate.

branches.path: Select the provider on the current Asana login page and complete its external authorization.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces email authentication, but the provider grant and return-to-Asana sequence are not publicly enumerated.

branches.condition: Use email or an organization authentication policy
branches.path: Continue with email and follow the applicable Asana password, organization SAML, or other domain-controlled route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Can add verification, organization membership, team join, or administrator policy before console access.

branches.condition: An account and workspace already exist
branches.path: Open My Settings > Apps > Manage Developer Apps, create and copy a named PAT, retrieve the workspace GID, then use cURL to create `Sample task`, GET it, rename it `Modified task`, and DELETE it.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Reaches the Quick start terminal after the blocked account and workspace transition.

branches.condition: Avoid operating on an existing workspace
branches.path: Request an Asana developer sandbox and use that workspace when it becomes available.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a sandbox request and access dependency before token and GID setup.

friction_gates.description: The Quick start requires an Asana identity and workspace before token creation.
The current login page exposes Google, Microsoft, and email entry, and organization policy can alter the applicable route.
The first task must target a workspace visible to the authenticated user, or a separately requested developer sandbox.
The developer console displays the new PAT only once, and the guide requires secure storage.
The create-task request requires the selected workspace GID.

sources.title: Build an app with Asana
evidence_supported: REST API as primary platform feature
Developer console and API request sequence

sources.title: Quick start guide
evidence_supported: Task CRUD commands and response signals

sources.title: Asana account: how to create it & log in | Help Center
evidence_supported: Email, Google, invitation, and SAML branches

sources.title: Log in - Asana
evidence_supported: Current Google, Microsoft, and email choices

uncertainties.question: Which current no-account authentication and workspace route reaches the Asana developer console without an undocumented transition?
uncertainties.impact: The developer cannot create the PAT or obtain a usable workspace GID until the identity and workspace route converges.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current login choices and help-center account branches do not provide one atomic path through provider authorization or email verification, organization or workspace setup, and console return.

excluded_after_success: Keeping the sample task, creating projects or portfolios, OAuth, public app distribution, webhooks, app components, deployment, App Directory review, pagination, and production error handling occur after the Quick start CRUD terminal.
